meta.title: NFS server in LXC | ProxMenux Documentation
meta.description: Expose folders over NFS from inside a Proxmox LXC container with ProxMenux. Auto-installs nfs-kernel-server, sets up a universal sharedfiles group convention, manages /etc/exports, network ACL and uninstall. Requires a privileged container.
meta.ogTitle: NFS server in LXC | ProxMenux Documentation
meta.ogDescription: Run an NFS server inside a privileged LXC container. Universal sharedfiles group, network ACL and full uninstall.

header.title: NFS server in LXC
header.description: Run an NFS kernel server inside a Proxmox LXC container and expose folders to other machines on the network. ProxMenux installs nfs-kernel-server, sets up a universal sharedfiles group convention so multiple privileged CTs can share files cleanly, manages /etc/exports and offers a full uninstall path.

privReq.title: Privileged container required
privReq.body: nfs-kernel-server needs to mount the kernel filesystem nfsd at /proc/fs/nfsd, which requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN in the host kernel namespace — not just in the container's user namespace. Unprivileged LXC does not expose that capability. In practice, the service simply fails to start with rpc.nfsd: Unable to access /proc/fs/nfsd errno 2 (No such file or directory) and systemd marks nfs-server.service as a failed dependency. The script enforces a privileged CT and aborts if it is unprivileged. If you cannot use a privileged CT, run the NFS server inside a VM.

what.heading: What this does
what.body: This is the opposite of the NFS client page. The container becomes an NFS server: it exposes a folder of its filesystem to clients on the network. Other CTs, the Proxmox host, VMs or physical machines can then mount that folder.
what.diagramServerDetail:
/mnt/data
(folder you expose)

chown root:sharedfiles
chmod 2775 (SGID)

nfs-kernel-server
+ rpcbind running
what.diagramClientDetail:
another CT, the host,
a VM, a physical machine…

shared.heading: The "sharedfiles" group convention
shared.body: Before exporting the folder, ProxMenux creates a group called sharedfiles with GID 101000 inside the container, adds every regular user to it, then sets the export directory to root:sharedfiles with mode 2775. The 2 at the front is the SGID bit — every file or folder created inside automatically inherits the sharedfiles group.
shared.gidBody: It maps to host GID 1000 when an unprivileged container reads the same file (LXC default idmap shifts everything by +100000). In the current ProxMenux flow the NFS server itself runs in a privileged CT (no shift on its side), but the convention keeps the group ID numerically consistent with unprivileged client CTs that may mount this share later. Two privileged CTs both using sharedfiles at GID 101000 can read / write each other's files cleanly because the GID numbers match end-to-end.
shared.remapTitle: The script also creates 'remap_*' users — they are vestigial here
shared.remapBody: For every regular user in the CT (and for common UIDs like 33 = www-data, 1000, 1001, 1002), the script creates a parallel remap_<uid> user with UID = <uid> + 100000, all members of sharedfiles. The +100000 shift mimics the LXC unprivileged idmap, but since this script enforces a privileged CT (no shift), those remap_* users are shadow accounts with no real-world counterpart on the host. They are harmless leftovers from a more ambitious design intent. If you don't see them in getent passwd, nothing breaks.

defaults.heading: Default export options — read this first
defaults.warnBody: ProxMenux defaults to rw,sync,no_subtree_check,'<'strong'>'no_root_squash'<'/strong'>'. This means any client root user can write as root on the export — appropriate for a trusted home LAN but never for an untrusted network. If your CT is reachable from an untrusted segment (a public network, a VPS, a hostile VLAN), change the export options to root_squash in the custom-options dialog.

opening.heading: Opening the tool
opening.body: From ProxMenux's main menu, open Storage & Share Manager → Configure NFS Server in LXC (only privileged). ProxMenux first asks you to pick the target CT (and starts it if stopped); aborts if unprivileged. Once the CT is selected you see this sub-menu with five options:
opening.imageAlt: NFS Server Manager menu — Create / View / Delete / Status / Uninstall

network.heading: Network ACL — who can mount the share
network.intro: The network field in /etc/exports filters which clients are allowed to mount. ProxMenux offers three modes:

rows.whenRich: Standard home / SOHO LAN. Covers every 192.168.*.* address.

rows.when: When your LAN is not in 192.168.x.x or you want a tighter scope.
Only one specific machine should mount. Most restrictive.

rows.effect: Allow read-write or read-only access for connecting clients.

rows.effectRich: Reply to write requests only after the data is on disk. Safer than async at the cost of throughput.

rows.effect: Skip the per-request check that the file is still inside the exported subtree. Faster and avoids issues when files are renamed mid-flight.

rows.effectRich: Trust client root. A client mounting as root writes as root on the server. Good for trusted LANs (e.g. backup tooling needs to preserve ownership). Replace with root_squash if you don't fully trust every machine on the network ACL.

manual.heading: Manual equivalent
manual.body: Replicate the whole flow by hand — every command runs inside the CT via pct exec <ctid> -- or pct enter <ctid>:

view.heading: View current exports
view.body: Cats /etc/exports from inside the CT (skipping comments / blanks) and prints each export with its network ACL and option string. Useful to check which folders are exposed before sharing the CT's IP with someone.

delete.heading: Delete an export
delete.body: Lists every line in /etc/exports for selection, removes the chosen one (sed -i), runs exportfs -ra and restarts nfs-kernel-server. The folder itself and its contents are left intact.

status.heading: Check NFS status
status.body: Diagnostic pass: confirms nfs-kernel-server and rpcbind are installed and active, prints exportfs -v output, lists active NFS sessions (showmount -a) and current client connections.

uninstall.heading: Uninstall NFS server
uninstall.body: Full clean-up after confirmation: stops + disables nfs-kernel-server and rpcbind, clears /etc/exports, apt-get purge the NFS packages, removes the sharedfiles group and the remap_* users, kills any leftover processes. The exported folders themselves are not deleted — only the NFS configuration and packages.
uninstall.warnTitle: The script stops at the export line, not at the data
uninstall.warnBody: Both Delete export and Uninstall NFS server remove the export configuration. The data on the exported folder is preserved. To delete the data too, do it explicitly with rm -rf after the script finishes — and back it up first if anyone might still need it.

troubleshoot.heading: Troubleshooting
troubleshoot.privBody: The selected CT is unprivileged. nfs-kernel-server cannot start there because mounting /proc/fs/nfsd needs CAP_SYS_ADMIN in the host kernel namespace, and the nfsd module is not exposed to the container's namespace either (modprobe nfsd from inside returns FATAL: Module nfsd not found). If you bypass the gate, you will see rpc.nfsd: Unable to access /proc/fs/nfsd errno 2 (No such file or directory) in the journal and no NFS ports will ever open. The only workable options are: convert the CT to privileged, or move the NFS server to a VM.
troubleshoot.aptIntro: The script assumes a Debian-family CT. On Alpine / Arch / Rocky / Alma, install the NFS server packages by hand:
troubleshoot.aptOutro: Then re-run the ProxMenux script — the install step skips when the tools are already present.
troubleshoot.aclTitle: Client cannot mount: 'access denied by server'
troubleshoot.aclBody: The client's IP is outside the network ACL you configured. Re-create the export with a wider subnet, or add the client's exact IP. Common gotcha: clients connecting through a router NAT may appear with the router's WAN IP, not the client's LAN IP — check on the server with tcpdump -n port 2049.
troubleshoot.ownTitle: Files written by the client appear with weird ownership on the server
ownItems: With no_root_squash (default), client root writes as root on the server. Files are owned by root:sharedfiles thanks to the SGID on the folder.

Non-root client users write as their own UID/GID. If their UID does not exist on the server, files appear with raw numbers (e.g. 1234:1234). Use the sharedfiles group on the client too, or align UIDs across the systems that share files.
troubleshoot.noShowTitle: Server reachable but showmount returns nothing
troubleshoot.noShowBody: After editing /etc/exports, you must reload the export table with exportfs -ra and restart nfs-kernel-server — the script does both, but if you edited the file by hand, do it yourself. Also confirm the firewall on the CT (and on the Proxmox host) allows TCP/UDP 2049 and the rpcbind port (111).

items.label: NFS share as Proxmox storage
items.tailRich: — once your CT is exporting, register that NFS share in Proxmox so it appears under Datacenter → Storage.
